narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we can support right now—it's not about doubting your heart, but about patterns in the data that shaped a cautious picture. At the top of what stood out was your Year 3 decile of 2, which was the strongest signal pulling toward a higher chance of bar failure, suggesting that even as you progressed, staying in the lower rankings within your school raised flags about handling the intense legal demands ahead. Your LSAT score of 25 followed close behind as a major influence in the same direction, painting you as someone who might struggle with the razor-sharp analytical demands of bar prep despite your determination. Layered on that was your Year 1 decile of 2, reinforcing early academic hurdles that the model saw as a persistent challenge. Your Asian background played a role too, as the model noted certain trends there contributing to the risk profile. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 added to this, hinting at foundational gaps that could compound under bar pressure. With low family income, the model worried about extra financial strains pulling focus away from studies. Choosing part-time status nudged it further, as juggling work often tips the scales toward tougher outcomes in these predictions. Even your gender as female factored in subtly, aligning with patterns the model weighed toward caution. Together, these elements—your deciles, LSAT, and the surrounding circumstances—created a 51.5% predicted chance of not passing the bar, which is why we had to say no to protect both you and our program's success rates. This isn't the end of your story; it's a snapshot urging you to target those deciles and scores with everything you've got—you've got the fire, and targeted improvements could rewrite this narrative entirely. Hang in there; law needs voices like yours.
